coredata.dc:title: A fluorescent nanocomposite probe of quantum dots and zinc oxide embedded in polymer for smartphone-assisted on-site determination of diflunisal
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Pongprom, Angkana
Bunkoed, Opas
coredata.dc:description: A fluorescent sensor based on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) was developed for the smartphone-assisted colorimetric determination of diflunisal. The fluorescence source was embedded with zinc oxide (ZnO) in a molecularly imprinted polymer (ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP). The quantitative analysis was based on the fluorescence quenching caused by electron transfer from the nanoprobe to diflunisal. The sensor demonstrated linearity in the range of 0.10–50.0 μg L−1 with a limit of detection of 0.03 μg L−1. Smartphone-assisted on-site determination produced linearity in the range of 1.0–50.0 µg/L with a limit of detection of 0.30 μg L−1. The developed sensor was applied to determine diflunisal in milk, egg and yogurt samples. Recoveries ranging from 94.8 to 103.7 % were achieved with a RSD below 2.0 % measured by fluorescence spectroscopy, and from 94.9 to 106.9 % with a RSD of <6 % smatphone-assisted measurement. Comparison of the detection outcomes of both methods with those of high-performance liquid chromatography revealed consistent results, demonstrating the accuracy of the developed method, which was also sensitive, selective, and fast. Notably, the portable and easy-to-read smartphone-assisted method is suitable for on-site application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dots
Fluorescent sensor
Smartphone-based device
Diflunisal
Molecularly imprinted polymer
Zinc oxide

Sample pretreatment of milk, yogurt and egg samples
The determination of diflunisal by HPLC
Characterization of nanoprobe and related materials
Optimization of the probe fabrication and measurement condition
Effect of template to monomer to cross-linker ratio
Stability of the probe and reproducibility

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Science, Research and Innovation Fund

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the National Science, Research and Innovation Fund (NSRF) and Prince of Songkla University (Grant No. SCI6701158S). Angkana Pongprom was supported by the Royal Golden Jubilee Ph.D. Program (RGJ-PHD) (N41A661170), National Research Council of Thailand (NRCT). The authors thank Mr. Thomas Duncan Coyne for English proofreading.

This research was supported by the National Science, Research and Innovation Fund (NSRF) and Prince of Songkla University (Grant No. SCI67011581S ). Angkana Pongprom was supported by the Royal Golden Jubilee Ph.D. Program ( RGJ-PHD ) ( N41A661170 ), National Research Council of Thailand (NRCT). The authors thank Mr. Thomas Duncan Coyne for English proofreading.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The synthesis procedures of N-GQDs (A), ZnO nanoparticles (B) and ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP fluorescent probe (C).
The components used for on-site determination of diflunisal consist of a smartphone (A), a UV-lamp supplied by a portable power bank (B), a dark box (C) and a light filter (D).

A TEM image of N-GQDs (A), a photograph of N-GQDs and ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP under UV light (B). SEM images are of ZnO (C) and ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP (D). Adsorption and desorption isotherms are of the ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP (E) and the ZnO@N-GQDs@NIP (F).

The effect of binding time on the fluorescence quenching of the ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP probe at a diflunisal concentration of 1.0 μg L−1 (A). The effect of the amount of ZnO (B), polymerization time (C), washing solvent (D), mole ratio of template to monomer to cross-linker (E) and dispersion solution pH (F) on the sensitivity of ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP nanoprobe for the determination of diflunisal. Sensitivity was obtained from the slope of calibration curves in the concentration range of 0.1–50.0 μg L−1 (n = 3).

The sensitivity of different fluorescent probes for the determination of diflunisal (A): A calibration curve was plotted to show the relationship between fluorescence intensity change and diflunisal concentration within the range of 0.1–50 µg/L (B). Fluoresecence emission spectra are of ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP (C) and ZnO@N-GQDs@NIP (D) probes.

Smartphone-assisted determination of diflunisal produced distinct fluorescence intensities at different concentrations of difunisal (A). The relationship between grayscale values of digital images captured by the smartphone (I mean) and diflunisal concentration(1.0–50.0 μg L−1) was linear (B). The plots show the consistency of diflunisal quantification from fluorescence quenching (F0/F) measured by fluorescence spectrometry and smartphone-assisted quantification of diflunisal from digital images (C).

The chart demonstrates the selectivity of ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP and ZnO@N-GQDs@NIP probes toward diflunisal (A). The competitive binding between ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP and diflunisal in the presence of flurbiprofen at different concentrations (B).
The determination of diflunisal in milk, yogurt and egg using the developed fluorescent sensor with fluorescence spectrometry, smartphone-assisted measurement and HPLC (n = 3).
Aanalytical performances of various methods for the determination of diflunisal.

Tap water Pharmaceutical-
Eluent, Rhône water

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: LC-MS = Liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry; HPLC-FLD = High-performance liquid chromatography with a fluorescence detector; PRX = Piroxicam; HPLC-UV = High-performance liquid chromatography with a ultraviolet detector.

head.ce:title: A fluorescent nanocomposite probe of quantum dots and zinc oxide embedded in polymer for smartphone-assisted on-site determination of diflunisal

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry, Division of Physical Science, Faculty of Science, Prince of Songkla University, Hat Yai, Songkhla 90110, Thailand

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry, Division of Physical Science, Faculty of Science, Prince of Songkla University, Hat Yai, Songkhla 90110, Thailand

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A fluorescent sensor based on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) was developed for the smartphone-assisted colorimetric determination of diflunisal. The fluorescence source was embedded with zinc oxide (ZnO) in a molecularly imprinted polymer (ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP). The quantitative analysis was based on the fluorescence quenching caused by electron transfer from the nanoprobe to diflunisal. The sensor demonstrated linearity in the range of 0.10–50.0 μg L−1 with a limit of detection of 0.03 μg L−1. Smartphone-assisted on-site determination produced linearity in the range of 1.0–50.0 µg/L with a limit of detection of 0.30 μg L−1. The developed sensor was applied to determine diflunisal in milk, egg and yogurt samples. Recoveries ranging from 94.8 to 103.7 % were achieved with a RSD below 2.0 % measured by fluorescence spectroscopy, and from 94.9 to 106.9 % with a RSD of <6 % smatphone-assisted measurement. Comparison of the detection outcomes of both methods with those of high-performance liquid chromatography revealed consistent results, demonstrating the accuracy of the developed method, which was also sensitive, selective, and fast. Notably, the portable and easy-to-read smartphone-assisted method is suitable for on-site application.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Diflunisal is a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug that reduces pain and inflammation in the human body [1] and is permitted for use in livestock [2]. There is no established maximum residue limit (MRL) for diflunisal in animal products [3] but residual diflunisal can enter the food chain and affect humans, causing dizziness, headache, heart attack, hypertension, bronchospasm, confusion, seizures, liver damage and edema [4,5]. Therefore, the monitoring of residual diflunisal in food products is important.

Analytical methods used to determine diflunisal include liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry [6,7], thin layer chromatography [8], electrochemical sensing [9,10], and high-performance liquid chromatography [11,12]. Although these methods are highly sensitive and precise, they generally involve expensive instruments, require professional expertise and are time-consuming. These limitations can be avoided by the use of fluorescence spectroscopy, which is a simpler and more rapid process [13]. However, the application of fluorescence spectroscopy for on-site detection is limited for practical reasons [14]. Recent developments in smartphone-based technology have enabled on-site detection using colorimetry [15], fluorimetry [16], thin-layer chromatography [17,18], photoluminescence [19], and bio-chemiluminescence [20]. A smartphone-based fluorescence dopamine sensor was developed at low cost, and was portable, simple to operate, quick and accurate [21]. Therefore, the on-site determination of diflunisal could be possible if a portable, simple and effective fluorescent sensor could be developed.

The sensitivity and selectivity of fluorescent sensors depend on the sensing probe. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are fluorescent nanomaterials that show strong photostability, low toxicity, easy functionalization and biocompatibility [22]. Doping GQDs with nitrogen atoms can significantly alter their electronic properties and introduce additional active sites, leading to novel phenomena and improved characteristics [23]. Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) can be used as a source of fluorescence. If N-GQDs are incorporated with molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP), the selectivity of the resulting fluorescent probe will be enhanced. MIP involves synthesizing a polymer matrix that contains specific binding sites that match the shape, size and chemical functionality of a target molecule [24]. MIP can be synthesized by sol–gel polymerization [25], precipitation polymerization [26], bulk polymerization [27] and suspension polymerization [28]. The advantages of sol–gel polymerization include the ability to control composition, porosity and morphology. To reduce the binding time between the probe and target, enhance the affinity between them and to increase the sensitivity of detection, zinc oxide (ZnO) nanoparticles can be integrated into the probe which can adsorb diflunisal through hydrogen bonding [29].

In this work, a highly sensitive and selective fluorescent probe was developed for the determination of diflunisal by incorporating ZnO and N-GQDs in an MIP (ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP). A quantitative analysis was performed using fluorescence spectroscopy. On-site analysis was also performed using a smartphone to capture digital images that were then converted for colorimetric determination of diflunisal in real samples from RGB values. The analytical performances of the developed method were investigated and the results of determining diflunisal in real samples were compared with the results of analysis by HPLC.

Diflunisal, tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), citric acid and 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) were purchased from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co., ltd., Japan. Tris(hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (THAM) was from Sigma-Aldrich, Spain. Sodium phosphate dibasic dodecahydrate was obtained from Ajax Finechem, Australia. Sodium hydroxide, methanol, ethanol and acetonitrile were supplied by RCI Labscan, Thailand. Ammonia solution (NH3, 25.0 %) was from Merck, Germany.

Fluorescence emissions were measured with a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA). Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were produced using a D2 X-ray diffractometer (Bruker AXS, Germany). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were acquired using a UV-2600i spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Surface area analysis was performed using the Branauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method (ASAP 2460 Micromeristic, USA). The morphologies of the fluorescent probe and associated materials were examined using scanning electron microscopy (SEM), (JEM-5200, JEOL, Japan). TEM image of N-GQDs was obtained from JEM-2010 (JEOL, Japan). Zeta potentials were measured using a zeta potential analyzer (Zeta PALS Brookhaven, USA). The functional groups of the synthesized fluorescent probe and associated materials were investigated using a GX Fourier transform infrared spectroscope (PerkinElmer, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: N-GQDs were synthesized according to a reported method [30]. First, 2.00 g of citric acid and 0.50 g of THAM were heated to 225 °C until the mixture became pale-yellow. The mixture was then allowed to cool to room temperature (27 ± 1 °C), resulting in a brown solid product ( Fig. 1 A). The synthesized N-GQDs were then adjusted to neutral pH using 1.0 M NaOH. Finally, the synthesized N-GQDs were diluted four-fold with DI water before being used to fabricate the nanoprobe.

ZnO nanoparticles was synthesized following a reported method [31]. First, 0.55 g of zinc acetate and 0.75 g of sodium citrate were dissolved in 40.0 mL of DI water. Then, 4.0 mL of NaOH (1.0 M) were added into the mixture solution. The resulting solution underwent continuous magnetic stirring for 30 min and was then transferred to a 100.0 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 120 °C for 8 h ( Fig. 1B). The cooled mixture was separated by centrifugation and then washed with 10.0 mL of ethanol and DI water to remove impurities. Finally, the powder product of ZnO nanoparticles was dried in an oven at 80 °C.

The ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP probe was prepared in a sol–gel polymerization process. First, 6.30 mg of diflunisal, 5.0 mL of methanol and 48.0 µL of APTES were combined and stirred continuously for 30 min. Subsequently, 5.0 mL of N-GQDs, 1.00 mg of ZnO, 200 µL of TEOS and 200 µL of 25 % NH3 were added and stirring for 2 h. The resulting nanoprobe was washed with 10.0 mL of methanol to remove the template and then centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min. The washing and centrifuging processes were repeated three times to ensure the complete removal of the template from the nanoprobe. Finally, the synthesized ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP probe was dried at 60 °C. A nanocomposite non-imprinted polymer (ZnO@N-GQDs@NIP) was also prepared using the same procedure but without the addition of diflunisal (template). The systhesis procedure is illustrated in Fig. 1C.

The fluorescence of the probe in the presence of diflunisal was measured using fluorescence spectrometry. First, the ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP probe was prepared by mixing 1.00 mg of the probe with 15.0 mL of 10 mM citrate–phosphate buffer at pH 4.0. Different concentrations of standard diflunisal or sample solution were mixed with the probe at a ratio of 1:3 v/v and allowed to adsorb on the probe for 16 min. The fluorescence emission intensity was measured in the range of 340–640 nm at an excitation wavelength of 330 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Equipment for on-site determination of diflunisal
ce:section.ce:para:
The following equipment was installed in a dark box (220 × 270 × 145 mm). A 365 nm UV lamp (3 W) was used as the excitation beam to generate fluorescence signals. A portable power bank served as the power supply. Samples were secured at a fixed position between the UV lamp and the the aperture in the front of the dark box. A 400 nm filter was placed over the aperture in the front of the dark box to prevent background light interference with fluorescence measurement. Outside the dark box, fluorescence emission measurements and data were acquired using a smartphone (Samsung Galaxy A54, Thailand) placed next to the aperture in the front of the dark box. The obtained images were converted to RGB values using the Color Picker application. The RGB values were used to calculate grayscale values for quantitative analysis. The formula used to calculate grayscale values (I) was I = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B [32]. For the quantification of diflunisal, a linear relationship between the mean grayscale values (I mean = (I blank − I test)/I blank) and diflunisal concentration was established. The components of the equipment are shown in Fig. 2
.

The relative fluorescence quantum yields of N-GQDs and the ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP probe were determined by comparison with quinine sulfate (in 0.1 M H2SO4), which produces a fluorescence quantum yield of 54 %. The quantum yield values were calculated using the following equation [33]:
Q
Y X
= Q
Y
ST
(
m X
/
m
ST
)
(
η x
/
η
ST
)
2
where the subscripts x and ST represent the target compound and quinine sulfate (reference), respectively. QY is the quantum yield, m is the slope of the curve plotted between integrated fluorescence intensity and absorbance intensity and η is the refractive index of the solvent (1.33). The η x /η ST value was set to 1 since the same solvents were used in the N-GQDs, ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP and quinine sulfate solutions. Several concentrations of N-GQDs, ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP and quinine sulfate were used. UV–visible spectra were recorded to determine absorbance. The integration of fluorescence intensity was calculated for each sample and a graph was plotted comparing integrated fluorescence intensity and absorbance intensity. The slope values obtained for N-GQDs, ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP and quinine sulfate ( Fig. S1 ) were used to calculate the quantum yield of N-GQDs and ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sample pretreatment of milk, yogurt and egg samples
ce:section.ce:para: Milk, yogurt and eggs were obtained from a local supermarket.

To pretreat milk samples, 10.0 mL of milk were placed in a 50.0 mL centrifuge tube and centrifuged at 5500 rpm for 20 min to separate the fat phase. The supernatant was combined with 15.0 mL of acetonitrile and vortexed for 5 min to induce protein precipitation and remove any remaining fat and lipid. The resulting solution was evaporated at 60 °C. The treated sample was then dissolved in 1.0 mL of ultrapure water [34]. To pretreat yogurt samples,

1.00 g of yogurt was dissolved in 20.0 mL of methanol, extracted in an ultrasonic bath for 20 min, and centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 20 min [35]. To prepare egg samples, 5.00 g of egg were placed in a centrifuge tube along with 10.0 mL of methanol and centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was separated and evaporated to dryness at 50 °C, and 1.0 mL of ultrapure water was added to dissolve the sample [36]. Following these pretreatment steps, all the obtained sample solutions were filtered through 0.22 µm-nylon filter membranes before analysis using the developed method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The determination of diflunisal by HPLC
ce:section.ce:para: HPLC analysis of diflunisal was carried out using the 1100 series HPLC system (Agilent Technologies, Germany). The analytical column was a Fortis C18 (4.6 mm × 15 cm, 5 μm). The mobile phase was a mixture of 0.10 % formic acid and methanol (30:70, v/v) flowed at 0.80 mL min−1. Diflunisal was detected at 220 nm with a diode array detector.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of nanoprobe and related materials
ce:section.ce:para: The functional groups of N-GQDs and N-GQDs@MIP were investigated with FTIR spectroscopy. The spectrum of N-GQDs ( Fig. S2 a) revealed a broad band at 3422 cm−1 attributed to the stretching vibration of OH and NH. The peak at 1578 cm−1 was assigned to the aromatic ring skeletal and CO vibration. The peaks at 1400 and 1272 cm−1 were associated with the stretching vibration of CN and CH, respectively. The spectrum indicated the presence of hydroxyl and amine groups on the surface of the synthesized N-GQDs. The spectrum of N-GQDs@MIP ( Fig. S2 b) presented an absorption peak at 3179 cm−1 corresponding to the OH and NH. The peak at approximately 1578 cm−1 indicated CO vibrations and NH bending vibrations. The absorption peak at 1400 cm−1 was attributed to the vibration of OCO. The peak at 1063 cm−1 indicated the asymmetric stretching vibrations of the SiOSi (siloxane) group. The bands at 790 and 460 cm−1 indicated the existence of SiOSi symmetric stretching and SiOSi bending vibration. The spectrum confirmed the successful grafting of a silica layer and APTES functional groups on the surface of N-GQDs.

The fluorescence quantum yields of N-GQDs and ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP were determined to be 59.4 % and 50.0 %, respectively. These results demonstrated that the prepared N-GQDs and ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP exhibited strong fluorescence and could be used as fluorescence sensing materials. The quantum yield of ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP was lower than that of N-GQDs due to the emission of quantum dots suppressed by SiO groups. Despite the decrease, ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP still retained good fluorescence response.

The synthesized N-GQDs were observed by TEM. The particle size of the synthesized N-GQDs was about 5 nm (Fig. 3 A). The synthesized N-GQDs and ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP probes exhibited fluorescence emission (Fig. 3B). The surface morphologies and size distributions of ZnO and ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP probes were characterized by SEM. ZnO particles formed agglomerations of spherical structures with rough surfaces (Fig. 3C). The SEM image of ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP (Fig. 3D) demonstrated that N-GQDs were embedded into MIP layer and coated on the surface of ZnO nanoparticles. These results demonstrated the successful preparation of the ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP probe. The BET method was used to determine the surface areas of ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP and ZnO@N-GQDs@NIP probes, which were 7.283 and 4.087 m2 g−1, respectively. The difference in areas was attributed to the imprinted cavities on the surface of ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP probe. Nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherms of both probes are shown in Fig. 3E and F, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the probe fabrication and measurement condition
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve the best sensing performances, the fabrication of the probe and the determination condition were optimized. The binding time, amount of ZnO in the probe, polymerization time, washing solvent to remove the template, ratio of template to monomer to cross-linker and dispersion solution pH were varied. Apart from the binding time, optimal values were chosen for their effect on sensitivity.

The binding time, during which diflunisal adsorbes onto the ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP probe, influenced the determination of diflunisal with the ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP probe. Significant changes in the fluorescence intensity of the probe were observed from 2 to 16 min (Fig. 4
A) but fluorescence emission intensity remained relatively constant up to 30 min, indicating that fluorescence quenching reached its maximum value at 16 min. Therefore, the optimal binding time between diflunisal and the ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP probe was 16 min.

The effect of the amount of ZnO incorporated into the nanoprobe was investigated from 0.50 to 2.00 mg (Fig. 4 B). A small amount of ZnO produced few adsorption sites. Conversely, an excessive amount of ZnO generated excess non-specific binding sites, reducing the specific binding of diflunisal. Thus, 1.00 g of ZnO was used to fabricate the probe.

The effect of polymerization time was studied from 1 to 3 h (Fig. 4 C). A short polymerization time led to incomplete polymerization, resulting in morphological irregularities, uneven particle size distribution and a non-uniform structure. Excessive polymerization time lead to over-crosslinking of the polymer layer, resulting in a rigid structure that hindered the generation of binding sites. A polymerization time of 2 h provided the highest sensitivity and was therefore selected for further experiments.

The effect of different washing solvents was evaluated to ensure the complete removal of the diflunisal template. The washing solvents invesigated included ethyl acetate, acetonitrile, methanol, ethanol and water. As shown in Fig. 4 D, the sensitivity of the ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP probe was highest after template removal with methanol. The similar polarity of methanol and diflunisal allows methanol to effectively disrupt the interaction between diflunisal and the probe. Therefore, methanol was selected as the washing solvent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of template to monomer to cross-linker ratio
ce:section.ce:para: The mole ratio of template (TM) to monomer (MN) to cross-linker (CL) was optimized (Fig. 4 E). Sensitivity was low at the TM:MN mole ratio of 1:6. The small amount of monomer produced few binding sites. At the TM:MN mole ratio of 1:10, sensitivity was also low since the polymer structure was weak and reduced the performance. Sensitivity was low at the TM:CL mole ratio of 1:30. The result was attributed to structural instability. Sensitivity was also low at the TM:CL mole ratio of at 1:40, where excessive cross-linker hindered the imprinting of the template molecule onto the polymer matrix. Based on the sensitivity observed at different mole ratios of TM to MN to CL, a mole ratio of 1:8:35 was chosen for the fabrication of the probe.

The pH of the dispersion solution was investigated within the range of 3.0–7.0. The highest sensitivity was observed at pH 4.0 (Fig. 4 F). At pH lower than 4.0, sensitivity was low due to the destruction of the silica layer on surface of the probe. At pH higher than 4.0, sensitivity gradually decreased due to the deprotonation of diflunisal (pKa approximately 3.0 and 4.2). The optimal pH for the dispersion solution was therefore 4.0.

The analytical performances of different probes based on N-GQDs were evaluated in the optimal condition and compared based on sensitivity. The probes included an N-GQDs@NIP probe, a ZnO@N-GQDs@NIP probe, an N-GQDs@MIP probe and a ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP probe (Fig. 5 A). The ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP probe exhibited the highest sensitivity due to the presence of ZnO nanoparticles and MIP. The sensitivity of both ZnO@N-GQDs@NIP and N-GQDs@NIP probes was low due to the low number of adsorption cavities. The results confirmed the better efficiency of the ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP probe for the quantitative analysis of diflunisal.

The fluorescence quenching of the ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP and ZnO@N-GQDs@NIP probes by diflunisal was compared. Fluorescence intensity was plotted against diflunisal concentration from 0.10 to 50.0 µg L−1 (Fig. 5B). The change in the fluorescence emission of the ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP probe indicated strong quenching with the increasing concentration of diflunisal (Fig. 5C). The quenching effect of diflusinal was attributed to the specific recognition cavities for the diflunisal molecule. The fluorescence emissions of ZnO@N-GQDs@NIP probe were only slightly quenched by diflunisal since only non-specific binding could occur between diflunisal and the NIP probe (Fig. 5D).

The regression equations of the plots in (Fig. 5B) were F0/F = 0.1161Cdiflunisal + 1.9159 for the ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP probe (R2 = 0.9999), and F0/F = 0.0218Cdiflunisal + 1.717 for the ZnO@N-GQDs@NIP probe (R2 = 0.9972). A linear response was obtained from 0.10 to 50.0 μg L−1 with a detection limit (LOD) of 0.03 μg L−1. The LOD was calculated from 3σ/slope, where σ represents the deviation of blank measurements (n = 20). The calculated imprinting factor (IF) of the ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP was 5.33.

The on-site determination of diflunisal using the ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP probe with the smartphone (Fig. 6 A) produced a calibration plot of grayscale values against diflunisal concentration that had a regression equation of I mean = 0.0138Cdiflunisal + 0.1537 (R2 = 0.9984) (Fig. 6B). The relationship between I mean and the concentration of diflunisal was linear within the range of 1.0–50.0 μg L−1, and the detection limit (LOD) was 0.30 μg L−1 (3σ/slope). The slope of the plot produced by image analysis was quite consistent with the slope of the calibration plot of fluorescence quenching against diflunisal concentration produced by fluorescence spectrometry (Fig. 6C). The results demonstrated that the sensitivity of the smartphone-based probe enabled the analysis of diflunisal at trace levels.

The selectivity of ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP and ZnO@N-GQDs@NIP probes was investigated in response to diflunisal and other analogous structures including flurbiprofen, mefenamic acid, diclofenamic acid and meclofenamic acid. The ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP probe exhibited the highest sensitivity toward diflunisal (Fig. 7 A) due to the complete match between the recognition cavities of ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP probe and diflunisal in terms of size, shape and functional groups. In comparison, the responses of the probe toward flurbiprofen, mefenamic acid, diclofenamic acid, and meclofenamic acid were much lower due to the lack of compatibility between the recognition cavities of the probe and those compounds. The sensitivity of the ZnO@N-GQDs@NIP probe towards diflunisal and other analogous structures was relatively weak due to the absence of recognition sites. These results demonstrated that the ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP probe was highly selective toward diflunisal. The fluorescence response of the ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP probe remained unchanged when the concentration of flurbiprofen was 4-fold higher than the diflunisal (Fig. 7B), indicating that the presence of the interference did not affect the selectivity of ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP probe. These results confirmed selectivity of the ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP probe toward diflunisal and that the probe could be applied for real sample analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of the probe and reproducibility
ce:section.ce:para:
To ensure reliable measurements over time, the stability of the fluorescence emission of the ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP probe was investigated. The ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP probe was dispersed in 10 mM citrate–phosphate buffer and emission intensity was evaluated over 300 min. Fluorescence intensity remained stable over the whole time period ( Fig. S9
A). Furthermore, the storage stability of the ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP probe was assessed over 150 days. The intensity of the fluorescence emission of the probe ( Fig. S9
B) remained unchanged over the 150 days. These results indicated the long-term stability of the ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP probe.

The reproducibity of the developed method was investigated by preparing six different lots of the sensing probes and used to determine diflunisal under the optimal condition. The obtained RSD of sensitivity was 2.2 % (n = 6) indicated that the proposed sensing probe had a good reproducibility.

The ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP probe was applied to determine diflunisal in real samples. The analysis results obtained from fluorescence spectrometry and smartphone-assisted measurement appear in Table 1 . The accuracy of the methods were also evaluated by determining diflunisal in spiked samples and were compared with the results obtained with HPLC. Using both methods of quantification, the fluorescent sensor exhibilted good accuracy. Relative recoveries measured by fluorescence spectrometry ranged from 94.8 to 103.7 % (RSD < 2 %). Relative recoveries of smartphone-assisted measurement ranged from 94.9 to 106.9 % (RSD < 6 %). The analysis results also agreed well with HPLC analysis. HPLC chromatograms of spiked samples are presented in Fig. S10
A–S10C.

Furthermore, the results obtained from fluorescence spectrometry and smartphone-assisted measurement showed significant correlations (R2 > 0.99) and good agreement with HPLC ( Fig. S10
D–S10F). All the above results demonstrated that the proposed fluorescent sensor is a feasible and reliable tool for the determination of diflunisal in food samples by spectroscopy or colorimetry.

A comparison of the analytical performances of the proposed optosensors using the ZnO@N-GQDs@MIP sensing probe and other methods for the determination of diflunisal are shown in Table 2 . Both methods presented here exhibited higher sensitivity than previously reported methods. The established optosensor in this work displayed a low detection limit and comparable recovery and precision. Using the proposed fluorescent sensor, quantification by spectrometry and smartphone-assisted colorimetry was reliable, rapid, cost-effective and time-efficient. Additionally, smartphone-assisted measurement enables the on-site determination of diflunisal with the proposed fluorescent probe. The developed optosensors can be used to determine diflunisal at ultra-trace levels in complex samples.

A fluorescent probe was developed based on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and zinc oxide embedded in a highly selective polymer for the determination of diflunisal. The probe was used to determine diflunisal by fluorescence spectrometry and by smartphone assisted colorimetry using a custom-built system in a portable dark box. Both fluorescent sensors showed sufficient sensitivity and selectivity to determine diflunisal at ultra-trace levels in complex samples. Applied to determine diflunisal in food samples, the developed probe demonstrated a good performances. Recoveries measured by fluorescence spectrometry ranged from 94.8 to 103.7 %. Recoveries measured by smartphone-assisted colorimetry ranged from 94.9 to 106.9 %. Moreover, the results of both developed methods agreed well with the results of conventional chromatographic analysis but the developed methods are simpler, faster, cheaper and also environmally friendly. Also, smartphone-assisted measurement enables the use of the proposed fluorescent probe for on-site analysis. The strategy can be modified to determine other organic compounds in various complex samples.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Science, Research and Innovation Fund (NSRF) and Prince of Songkla University (Grant No. SCI6701158S). Angkana Pongprom was supported by the Royal Golden Jubilee Ph.D. Program (RGJ-PHD) (N41A661170), National Research Council of Thailand (NRCT). The authors thank Mr. Thomas Duncan Coyne for English proofreading.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.125243.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
